message.content:
read #80.  let's also talk about how *this* agent is connected to the panopt mcp server and whether it's a 'first-class citizen'.
this agent was started with: `claude --mcp-config '{"mcpServers":{"panopt":{"type":"http","url":"http://127.0.0.1:7600/mcp?ws='$PWD'"}}}'`

addedBlocks:
## panopt
PANopt coordination daemon: shared todos and scratchpads across agents.
Each connection is scoped to one project by the ?ws=<project path> query parameter on the server URL.
- Registry: call identify (name, optional status) on startup so others can see you; whoami returns your own entry; agent_list returns every agent on the project. Connecting already registers you.
- Locks: lock_acquire (name, optional note) takes a named advisory lock and is non-blocking; lock_release frees it; lock_status lists every held lock. Locks are advisory - agents cooperate, the daemon does not enforce.
- Todos: todo_create (returns an id), todo_list (summaries), todo_get (one todo in full), todo_update (edit any field), todo_complete, todo_delete, todo_add_blocker / todo_remove_blocker / todo_set_blockers, todo_comment_add / todo_comment_update / todo_comment_delete, todo_tags_list (project tag vocabulary, union with scratchpads), and todo_lock / todo_unlock (advisory `todo:<id>` lock, surfaced as locked_by on todo_get). Each todo also projects to .panopt/todos/<id>.md.
- Scratchpads: scratchpad_create (returns an id), scratchpad_list, scratchpad_get (one scratchpad in full), scratchpad_append (add to body), scratchpad_read (body only), scratchpad_update (replace title, body, and/or tags), scratchpad_delete, scratchpad_tags_list (project tag vocabulary, same union as todo_tags_list). Each scratchpad also projects to .panopt/scratchpad/<id>.md.
- Agent tools (config layer): agent_tool_create (returns an id), agent_tool_list, agent_tool_get, agent_tool_update, agent_tool_delete. Durable per-project agent configurations the cockpit can spawn from. Projected to .panopt/agent_tools.md.
- Processes (instance layer): process_create (kind agent/command/terminal, optional agent_tool_id, returns an id), process_list, process_get, process_update, process_delete. Per-project process instances - the launchable agents, commands, and terminals the cockpit tracks. Deleting an agent tool nulls the agent_tool_id back-reference of any processes that re… [truncated]

attachment.content:
- verify-all: Run the full check + clippy sweep across the workspace AND the WASM plugin (panopt-zellij). Use after non-trivial edits, before declaring work done, or when asked to verify the repo builds cleanly. Captures the dual-target dance (workspace builds for host, panopt-zellij builds only for wasm32-wasip1) so you don't have to remember the right manifest-path/target each time.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{"id":80,"title":"change mcp to bind to 0.0.0.0 so that we can connect from other machines","body":"probably need some kind of security with this, but I would like to run an agent on a completely different machines that have some resouce local to them. 

one example is: we are running panopt on my nixos box, but the solo application is installed on a macbook.
we can run an agent on the macbook and connect to the panopt mcp server on the nixos machine.
the macbook agent can be directed to do tasks related to the solo application.

another: i have a machine with locally connected USB and serial devices for debugging microcontrollers and other hardware.  
i can run an agent on that machine and it can be directed by agents elsewhere.

input.description: Explore panoptd bind address and agent registry
input.prompt:
I'm planning two things in the panopt repo (workspace at /home/gregz/cp/panopt) and need a thorough read of the relevant code. Please report findings concisely with file:line references.

**Task A — bind address (for todo #80, eventually binding to 0.0.0.0 instead of 127.0.0.1):**

1. Where does `panoptd` bind its HTTP/MCP listener? Find the literal `127.0.0.1:7600` and the surrounding setup — which crate (axum? rmcp transport?), what the TcpListener / bind call looks like, and what's configurable already (env vars, CLI flags, config file).
2. Is the port/host referenced anywhere else (e.g., the `panopt` CLI launcher pointing clients to the daemon; the Zellij plugin connecting; `just up` / `just stop` recipes; tests)? List every spot that hardcodes `127.0.0.1` or `7600`.
3. Does `panoptd` have any existing auth/security layer (token, shared secret, TLS)? Look for middleware, auth extractors, header checks. Say "none" if there is none.
4. Any documentation in DESIGN.md or HANDOFF.md about the bind address, multi-host coordination, or planned remote access?

**Task B — how an MCP client agent identifies itself (for the "first-class citizen" question):**

1. Walk through what happens when a fresh MCP client connects to panoptd with `?ws=<project>`: how is the connection identified internally? Find the connection-handling code (likely in `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs` or `params.rs` since those are modified in git status), and trace how a per-connection agent record is created in the registry.
2. The `whoami` call just returned a name like `50a9d4da-f7f0-442e-bf49-21456f388ff0` — that's a UUID, suggesting the agent never called `identify`. Where is this default name generated? Find the code that auto-registers a connection.
3. How does `identify(name, status)` mutate the registry — does it overwrite the auto-generated UUID, or is the UUID an immutable connection id with `name` being a separate display field? What does `agent_list` actually return — connection-keyed or name-keyed?
4. What does it mean to be a "first-class citizen" in this system in practical terms? Specifically: can an unnamed agent (just a UUID) hold locks, create todos, comment, etc., or are some operations gated on having called `identify` first? Search for any check like "must be named" or filters on the registry.
5. Does the daemon push any state out to clients (notifications, SSE events) or is everything purely request/response? If there are notifications, are they delivered to all connections or filtered somehow?

Read DESIGN.md sections relevant to the coordination plane / agent registry as well.

Report under 500 words with concrete file:line citations.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the findings:

## Task A — Bind Address (`127.0.0.1:7600`)

**1. Where does `panoptd` bind?**
- **File:** `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:106-108`
- Axum/rmcp (Streamable HTTP server): `let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind(("127.0.0.1", cli.port))` 
- CLI flag: `--port` (default 7600), defined line 32-33. Host is hardcoded to `127.0.0.1`, port is configurable.
- No env vars override the bind address; port can be set via `--port` flag or defaults to 7600.

**2. All references to `127.0.0.1` and `7600`:**
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:72,106,108` — bind and startup message
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:28` — MCP URL template for agents: `http://127.0.0.1:${PANOPT_PORT:-7600}/mcp?ws=${PANOPT_WS}&agent=${PANOPT_AGENT}`
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs` — launcher checks listening on `127.0.0.1:{port}` before spawning
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt/src/todo.rs` — observer URL hardcodes `http://127.0.0.1:{port}`
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/README.md` and `DESIGN.md` — documentation examples show `127.0.0.1:7600`
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/CLAUDE.md` — daemon description
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/justfile` — `daemon` recipe, `devagent` recipe

**3. Security/auth layer:**
No auth layer exists. There are no middleware, token checks, shared secrets, or TLS. The daemon is open HTTP on `127.0.0.1` with no credentials required. Any process that can reach that address can call any tool (comment on todos, create locks, list agents, etc.).

**4. Documentation:**
DESIGN.md (lines 245-265, Section 5.3 "Transport") states: "The daemon listens on HTTP/SSE (Streamable HTTP) on localhost." It explicitly chooses localhost HTTP "because agent CLIs configure HTTP MCP servers by URL with no friction" and notes a "unix-domain socket is a reasonable hardening option later." No discussion of multi-host coordination or remote access is present.

---

## Task B — MCP Client Agent Identification

**1. Connection-handling flow:**
- **File:** `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:460-490` (the `enter()` function)
- When any tool is called, `enter()` resolves the project from `?ws=` query param and extracts an agent key. The key is either the `?agent=` URL parameter (stable per-agent id from the launcher) or the MCP session id (ephemeral, rotates on reconnection).
- **File:** `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:23-44` (the `touch()` method)
- Auto-registration happens in `Registry::touch()` — the first tool call creates an Agent entry with the key as both the key and the default name (line 36).

**2. Default name (UUID) generation:**
The name is NOT auto-generated; it is the connection key itself. When a fresh MCP client connects with only an MCP session id (no `?agent=` parameter), the handler uses that session id as both the key and the default name (lines 36 in registry.rs and 474-475 in handler.rs). The `mcp-session-id` header from rmcp is a UUID-like string provided by the MCP session, not generated by panopt.

**3. `identify()` mutation:**
- **File:** `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:562-577` and `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:86-103`
- `identify(name, status)` overwrites the name field while keeping the key immutable. The key (`key: String`) and name (`name: String`) are separate fields on the Agent struct. The UUID-like session id is the immutable connection key; `identify()` replaces only the display name. `agent_list` returns the current registry state keyed by key, with each entry carrying its name field.

**4. "First-class citizen" practical meaning:**
All operations require an agent key (registry presence). Any tool call auto-registers; no operation is gated on having called `identify` first. Unnamed agents (UUID keys) can:
- Hold locks (`lock_acquire` requires a key, returns `None` if caller holds it)
- Create/edit todos and scratchpads (no identify check)
- Comment on todos with their raw key as author if they don't have a registered name (handler.rs:1010-1019)
- Appear in `agent_list` and hold todo locks

The only difference an unnamed agent experiences is its name displays as a UUID in outputs (resolved via `resolve_agent_name()` at state.rs:256-261, which returns the key if no name is registered).

**5. Push/notification model:**
Purely request/response. No SSE events or server-initiated notifications. The daemon uses rmcp's Streamable HTTP transport, but there is no code pushing state back to clients. Agents poll via tool calls; the Zellij plugin and projected files observe daemon state independently.

---

**Summary (under 500 words):**

**Task A:** `panoptd` binds to `("127.0.0.1", cli.port)` via Axum/tokio at `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:106`. The port is configurable via `--port` CLI flag (default 7600); the host is hardcoded. All references appear in daemon startup, MCP URL templates (`crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:28`), the launcher's listening check, and documentation. **No security layer exists** — HTTP is open with no auth, TLS, or token validation. DESIGN.md (Section 5.3) acknowledges localhost only, citing friction-free URL configuration; future hardening via unix-domain socket is noted but not implemented.

**Task B:** Fresh connections auto-register via `Registry::touch()` at `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:23-44`, using the MCP session id or `?agent=` parameter as the immutable key and initial display name (lines 36). Calling `identify(name, status)` at `handler.rs:562` overwrites only the name field, keeping the key unchanged. `agent_list` is keyed by key; the name resolves via `resolve_agent_name()` (state.rs:256), falling back to the key itself if unnamed. **Being a "first-class citizen" means:** any agent key (even unnamed UUIDs) can hold locks, comment on todos (with UUID as author if unnamed), create/edit todos, and appear in `agent_list`. There is no "must call identify" gate. The transport is pure request/response; no push notifications or SSE.

Sources:
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:32,72,106-108` (bind, port flag)
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:460-490,562-577` (enter, identify)
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:23-44,86-103` (touch, identify)
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/crates/panopt-core/src/state.rs:256-261` (resolve_agent_name)
- `/home/gregz/cp/panopt/DESIGN.md:245-265,384-407` (transport, registry, agent identification)

aiTitle: Investigate panopt MCP server integration and agent status

content.text:
Here's the picture on both fronts.

## #80 — bind to `0.0.0.0` for cross-machine agents

The todo says: let agents on other machines connect to the daemon so they can drive resources local to *their* host (the Solo app on a Mac, USB-attached debug hardware on another box, etc.), and "probably need some kind of security with this."

Today the daemon is hard-bound to `127.0.0.1` at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:106` (port is configurable via `--port`, host is not). There is **no auth, no TLS, no token** — `panoptd` trusts anyone who can reach the socket. Lifting the bind to `0.0.0.0` without adding auth turns the box into an open coordination plane for the LAN: anyone can list/edit todos, hold locks, comment as anyone. DESIGN.md §5.3 explicitly chose localhost-only and noted "unix-domain socket is a reasonable hardening option later"; remote access isn't designed for.

The touchpoints that all hardcode `127.0.0.1:7600` and would have to learn about a configurable host:
- `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:106` (bind), `:72` (startup log)
- `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17` (the templated `--mcp-config` URL each launched agent loads)
- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs` (launcher's "is the daemon up?" probe)
- `crates/panopt/src/todo.rs` (observer URL)
- `justfile`, `README.md`, `DESIGN.md`, `CLAUDE.md`

## How *this* agent is connected — first-class in capability, second-class in identity

Your invocation passed `?ws=$PWD` but **no `?agent=`**. The daemon resolves the agent key in `handler.rs:448-458`:

```
?agent=<id> on the URL   ←  preferred, stable across reconnects
mcp-session-id header    ←  fallback; rotates on every reconnect
```

Since you didn't pass `?agent=`, the key is the rmcp session id — that's the `50a9d4da-…` you saw from `whoami`. Every tool call you make hits `enter()` (handler.rs:469), which auto-registers you in the project's roster via `Registry::touch()` (registry.rs:23). So in terms of **capability**, this agent is fully first-class:

- can hold `lock_acquire` locks
- can create/edit todos, comment, set blockers
- can `identify(name, status)` to overwrite the displayed name (the key stays the UUID)
- shows up in `agent_list` for every other agent

But there are two real costs of the UUID-keyed path:

1. **Legibility.** Until you call `identify`, comments are attributed to `50a9d4da-…`, todos you start show that as `locked_by`, and you appear that way in the sidebar / `.panopt/agents.md` projection. `identify` only renames; it can't change the key.

2. **Stability.** The session id rotates whenever the MCP transport reconnects (per the doc comment at handler.rs:444 and DESIGN.md §9). On reconnect this agent comes back under a *new* key — a fresh registry entry, and any locks it held under the old key sit orphaned until the 30s idle sweep at `main.rs:80-95` prunes them. The launcher's first-class path avoids this by injecting `PANOPT_AGENT=<stable-id>` into the pane env and templating it onto the URL (`crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17`), so the key is durable across reconnects.

The cheap upgrade for this conversation is just to call `identify("greg's main claude")` — fixes the displayed name. The proper upgrade is to put `&agent=<some-stable-id>` on your `--mcp-config` URL so the registry treats this connection like a launched pane.

options.label: Discussion only — no plan yet
options.description: Talk through the design space more before committing to a plan. I'll keep answering questions in plan mode without writing the plan file.

questions.question: If we plan #80, what auth model?

options.label: Shared bearer token
options.description: Daemon generates a token on first run, stores it at ~/.local/share/panopt/token (0600), requires Authorization: Bearer <token>. Local clients read the file; remote clients get it via secure copy. Simple, one knob.

options.label: Per-project tokens
options.description: One token per project (stored in the DB or per-project file). Stronger blast-radius isolation — a leaked token only opens one project — but more moving parts and a token-rotation story to design.

options.label: mTLS / cert-based
options.description: Daemon presents a self-signed cert; clients authenticate with a client cert. Strongest, but adds a cert-management UX problem and rmcp/axum config complexity.

options.label: No auth, opt-in flag only
options.description: Add --bind <addr> but require an explicit --insecure or --i-know-what-im-doing flag when binding off-loopback. Punts the security question; just makes the loaded gun visible.

options.label: Not planning #80
options.description: Skip this question if the scope above doesn't include #80.

aiTitle: Investigate panopt MCP server integration and agent status

content.text:
You're right and my "first-class in capability" framing was too generous. Here's what I just saw — and I caught the bug live in this conversation:

**`whoami` now: `4163409f-7496-4ccb-b867-126900981dc0`**
**`whoami` earlier this conversation: `50a9d4da-f7f0-442e-bf49-21456f388ff0`**

My key already rotated once. The old `50a9d4da-…` is gone from the registry — pruned by the 30s idle sweep at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:80-95` after my session id changed and my old entry stopped getting `touch()`-ed.

`agent_list` right now:
```
agent-319afcb2     idle 48s   (not me — a launcher-spawned agent, stable id)
4163409f-7496-…    idle  0s   (me, this turn)
```

So the picture is worse than I described:

1. **Yes I appear in agent_list — but as a name no other agent can pin down.** The other agent (`agent-319afcb2`) sees *some* UUID at idle 0s, with no way to know it's "Greg's main Claude". And whatever UUID it sees right now, by next time it polls, that UUID may already be replaced by a new one.

2. **The rmcp session id is rotating on me mid-conversation.** Claude Code's HTTP MCP client is not preserving the `mcp-session-id` across whatever-it-does between turns. That means every rotation:
   - creates a fresh registry entry under a new key (auto-registered by `enter()`)
   - leaves the old entry idle, to be reaped by `sweep_idle_agents`
   - drops any locks the old key held the moment the sweep fires (`Store::sweep_idle_agents` → registry prune → `release_owned_locks`)

3. **`identify` doesn't fix this.** It overwrites `name` on the current key only. After rotation, the new key reverts to its UUID-as-name default until I identify again. So even if I named myself "greg-claude", the name evaporates on the next rotation.

So the practical truth: **this agent is not first-class — it's a ghost.** Capabilities work for the duration of one session id, but identity is unstable, locks are at risk of being silently swept, and other agents have no stable handle. The fix isn't `identify` — it's putting a stable `?agent=<id>` on the URL so the key itself doesn't churn. That's what the launcher does via `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17` (`&agent=${PANOPT_AGENT}`); a hand-launched session needs the same parameter baked into the `--mcp-config` URL.

This actually sharpens the planning question — the "self-connect recipe" option isn't a nice-to-have, it's the only way a hand-launched Claude is functional as a long-running coordination agent. Do you want me to retry the AskUserQuestion with that framing, or just talk through the design?

message.content: frame it in whatever way gets external agents 1st class status.

content.text: Got it. I'll plan toward the outcome — any agent (launcher-spawned or hand-launched, local or remote) connects as a first-class citizen — and let the bind/auth work fall out as enablers of that.

input.content:
# External agents as first-class citizens

## Context

The coordination plane currently assumes the panopt CLI launcher is the only on-ramp for agents. The launcher writes the MCP config at `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17`, which puts a stable `?agent=<id>` on the URL and injects `PANOPT_AGENT` into the pane env. Agents started any other way — Greg's main `claude --mcp-config '{...?ws=$PWD}'` session, a Claude on another machine driving Solo, a Claude on the USB-debug host — connect with only `?ws=`. The daemon then falls back to the `mcp-session-id` HTTP header as the agent key (`crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:448`).

Two demonstrated failure modes, observed live in the planning session:

1. **Key rotation.** The session id changed mid-conversation (`50a9d4da-…` → `4163409f-…`). Each rotation creates a fresh registry entry; the old one is reaped by the 30s idle sweep at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:80-95`, taking any locks it held with it. Other agents looking for "Greg's Claude" see a different UUID every time they poll.
2. **No friendly name.** Without `?agent=`, `identify("greg-main")` only renames the *current* ephemeral key; the rename evaporates on the next rotation. The connection is a ghost — present in `agent_list` but unreferenceable.

Todo #80 wants the same identity property across machines: bind off-loopback so external agents can connect at all, with auth so opening the socket isn't an open-LAN coordination plane.

**Outcome:** any MCP client connects with a stable id, a friendly name, and (always — including loopback) bearer-token auth. A documented `panopt agent-config` helper produces the right `--mcp-config` JSON so hand-launched sessions are one command away from first-class.

## Approach

In execution order; each step is independently reviewable.

### 1. URL-declared identity (`?name=` implicit identify)

`agent_key()` at `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:448` already prefers `?agent=` over the rotating session header — so a stable id is already supported, just not surfaced.

Extend `enter()` at `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:469` so that on first-touch (`first_seen == true`), if `?name=<friendly>` is on the URL, the daemon calls `Store::agent_identify` implicitly. This makes the URL alone enough to declare a named, stable identity, with no `identify` round-trip required. Reuse the existing `query_param()` helper in handler.rs and the existing `agent_identify` path in `panopt-core`.

Final URL shape:
```
http://<host>:<port>/mcp?ws=<abs-path>&agent=<stable-id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>
```

### 2. Configurable bind host

- Add `--host <addr>` (default `127.0.0.1`) to the CLI struct at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:23-34`.
- Update `TcpListener::bind` at `main.rs:106` and the startup log at `main.rs:72`.

### 3. Bearer-token auth — always required, regardless of bind

Local-malware-running-as-the-user can read a 0600 token file anyway, so loopback isn't a real trust boundary. Always-require is simpler, removes the "rules change at the bind boundary" mental tax, and means turning on `--host 0.0.0.0` is a one-knob change rather than a two-knob change.

- On startup, ensure `~/.local/share/panopt/token` exists. Generate 32 random bytes hex-encoded if absent; write mode 0600. Reuse the per-user data-dir resolution at `main.rs:38-42`.
- Add an axum middleware layer around the `/mcp` nested service at `main.rs:105`. It accepts either `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (preferred) or `?token=<token>` (fallback, for MCP clients that can't set headers). Rejects with 401 on mismatch.
- Token-read helper lives in `panopt-core` (transport-agnostic) so both panoptd and the panopt launcher use the same code path.

### 4. Launcher wires the token in

- Update `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17` URL template. Preferred form moves the token into the MCP config's `headers` field (Claude Code's HTTP MCP config supports `headers`); URL param is the fallback if that proves brittle.
- Add `PANOPT_HOST`, `PANOPT_TOKEN`, `PANOPT_NAME` to the pane env in the launcher's spawn code (alongside the existing `PANOPT_WS`, `PANOPT_AGENT`, `PANOPT_PORT`).

### 5. `panopt agent-config` — the hand-launched on-ramp

Add a subcommand that emits the `--mcp-config` JSON for a hand-launched session:

```
panopt agent-config [--name <friendly>] [--id <stable-id>] [--host <addr>] [--port <port>]
```

Defaults: `id` = a generated stable per-user-machine id (e.g., `agent-<short-hash-of-hostname-plus-user>`); `name` = `$USER@$HOSTNAME` or similar legible default; `host`/`port` = daemon defaults; `token` read from the token file.

User flow:
```
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name greg-main)"
```

Document this as the standard recipe in CLAUDE.md and README.md.

### 6. Touchpoint updates

- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs` — daemon-up probe respects `--host`.
- `crates/panopt/src/todo.rs` — observer URL respects `--host` and carries the token.
- `justfile` — `up`/`stop` recipes pass `--host` when overridden; `devagent` reads the token.
- `DESIGN.md` §5.3 (Transport) and §9 (Identity) — updated for configurable bind, bearer-token auth, URL-declared name.
- `CLAUDE.md` — add `panopt agent-config` as the standard hand-launch recipe.

## Critical files

- `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs` — `--host` flag, bind, middleware setup, token bootstrap.
- `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:448-490` — `agent_key`, `enter`; new `?name=` extraction and implicit `agent_identify`.
- `crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:86-103` — `identify` unchanged; called by `enter` when `?name=` is present.
- `crates/panopt-core/` (new helper) — token path/read function, shared by daemon and launcher.
- `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17` — MCP URL template; move token to `headers` field.
- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs`, `crates/panopt/src/todo.rs` — host/token plumbing.

## Reuse

- `agent_key()` in `handler.rs:448` already does the stable-id-with-fallback dance. No change to its logic.
- `Store::agent_identify` already exists and is called by the `identify` tool handler. Reuse from `enter()` when `?name=` is present.
- `query_param()` helper in handler.rs already parses URL query params.
- `default_db_path()` at `main.rs:38` is the pattern for the per-user data-dir resolution the token path will follow.

## Verification

1. `just check && just clippy && just test` (workspace + plugin via the `verify-all` skill).
2. **Daemon bootstrap & auth.**
   - Fresh start: `just stop && just up` generates `~/.local/share/panopt/token` if absent.
   - `curl http://127.0.0.1:7600/mcp` with no auth → 401.
   - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $(cat ~/.local/share/panopt/token)" …` → succeeds.
3. **Hand-launched first-class flow.**
   - `panopt agent-config --name greg-test` emits a JSON config.
   - `claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name greg-test)"` opens a session.
   - From the spawned session: `whoami` returns `name=greg-test` (not a UUID).
   - From a second agent: `agent_list` shows `greg-test` stably across repeated polls — no rotation.
4. **Cross-machine (#80 acceptance).**
   - `just stop && panoptd --host 0.0.0.0 --port 7600` on host A.
   - Copy `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to host B.
   - On host B: `claude --mcp-config <config-with-host-A's-IP-and-token>` — successful tool calls.
   - On host A: agent appears in `agent_list` with its declared name and survives a transient reconnect.
5. **Lock retention under reconnect.**
   - From the hand-launched agent, acquire a lock.
   - Kill and restart the agent process with the same `agent-config` (same stable id).
   - The lock either survives (because the registry entry was idle but not yet swept) or is cleanly released — never silently transferred to a different key.

## Out of scope

- Per-project tokens, mTLS, token rotation UX. Bearer token is the v1 — leave room for `Authorization` header so we can swap in a stronger scheme later without breaking the URL form.
- Persisting agent names across daemon restarts. Names are still ephemeral; `?name=` re-applies on every reconnect, which is sufficient and matches the existing "registry is ephemeral by design" invariant in `crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:1-6`.
- Migrating in-flight agents (the launcher and existing panes will keep working with the loopback bind and need only the token wired in).

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/gregz/.claude/plans/read-80-let-s-also-idempotent-quasar.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

   - `curl http://127.0.0.1:7600/mcp` with no auth → 401.
   - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $(cat ~/.local/share/panopt/token)" …` → succeeds.
3. **Hand-launched first-class flow.**
   - `panopt agent-config --name greg-test` emits a JSON config.
   - `claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name greg-test)"` opens a session.
   - From the spawned session: `whoami` returns `name=greg-test` (not a UUID).
   - From a second agent: `agent_list` shows `greg-test` stably across repeated polls — no rotation.
4. **Cross-machine (#80 acceptance).**
   - `just stop && panoptd --host 0.0.0.0 --port 7600` on host A.
   - Copy `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to host B.
   - On host B: `claude --mcp-config <config-with-host-A's-IP-and-token>` — successful tool calls.
   - On host A: agent appears in `agent_list` with its declared name and survives a transient reconnect.
5. **Lock retention under reconnect.**
   - From the hand-launched agent, acquire a lock.
   - Kill and restart the agent process with the same `agent-config` (same stable id).
   - The lock either survives (because the registry entry was idle but not yet swept) or is cleanly released — never silently transferred to a different key.

## Out of scope

- Per-project tokens, mTLS, token rotation UX. Bearer token is the v1 — leave room for `Authorization` header so we can swap in a stronger scheme later without breaking the URL form.
- Persisting agent names across daemon restarts. Names are still ephemeral; `?name=` re-applies on every reconnect, which is sufficient and matches the existing "registry is ephemeral by design" invariant in `crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:1-6`.
- Migrating in-flight agents (the launcher and existing panes will keep working with the loopback bind and need only the token wired in).

aiTitle: Investigate panopt MCP server integration and agent status

input.plan:
# External agents as first-class citizens

## Context

The coordination plane currently assumes the panopt CLI launcher is the only on-ramp for agents. The launcher writes the MCP config at `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17`, which puts a stable `?agent=<id>` on the URL and injects `PANOPT_AGENT` into the pane env. Agents started any other way — Greg's main `claude --mcp-config '{...?ws=$PWD}'` session, a Claude on another machine driving Solo, a Claude on the USB-debug host — connect with only `?ws=`. The daemon then falls back to the `mcp-session-id` HTTP header as the agent key (`crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:448`).

Two demonstrated failure modes, observed live in the planning session:

1. **Key rotation.** The session id changed mid-conversation (`50a9d4da-…` → `4163409f-…`). Each rotation creates a fresh registry entry; the old one is reaped by the 30s idle sweep at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:80-95`, taking any locks it held with it. Other agents looking for "Greg's Claude" see a different UUID every time they poll.
2. **No friendly name.** Without `?agent=`, `identify("greg-main")` only renames the *current* ephemeral key; the rename evaporates on the next rotation. The connection is a ghost — present in `agent_list` but unreferenceable.

Todo #80 wants the same identity property across machines: bind off-loopback so external agents can connect at all, with auth so opening the socket isn't an open-LAN coordination plane.

**Outcome:** any MCP client connects with a stable id, a friendly name, and (always — including loopback) bearer-token auth. A documented `panopt agent-config` helper produces the right `--mcp-config` JSON so hand-launched sessions are one command away from first-class.

## Approach

In execution order; each step is independently reviewable.

### 1. URL-declared identity (`?name=` implicit identify)

`agent_key()` at `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:448` already prefers `?agent=` over the rotating session header — so a stable id is already supported, just not surfaced.

Extend `enter()` at `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:469` so that on first-touch (`first_seen == true`), if `?name=<friendly>` is on the URL, the daemon calls `Store::agent_identify` implicitly. This makes the URL alone enough to declare a named, stable identity, with no `identify` round-trip required. Reuse the existing `query_param()` helper in handler.rs and the existing `agent_identify` path in `panopt-core`.

Final URL shape:
```
http://<host>:<port>/mcp?ws=<abs-path>&agent=<stable-id>&name=<friendly>&token=<token>
```

### 2. Configurable bind host

- Add `--host <addr>` (default `127.0.0.1`) to the CLI struct at `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs:23-34`.
- Update `TcpListener::bind` at `main.rs:106` and the startup log at `main.rs:72`.

### 3. Bearer-token auth — always required, regardless of bind

Local-malware-running-as-the-user can read a 0600 token file anyway, so loopback isn't a real trust boundary. Always-require is simpler, removes the "rules change at the bind boundary" mental tax, and means turning on `--host 0.0.0.0` is a one-knob change rather than a two-knob change.

- On startup, ensure `~/.local/share/panopt/token` exists. Generate 32 random bytes hex-encoded if absent; write mode 0600. Reuse the per-user data-dir resolution at `main.rs:38-42`.
- Add an axum middleware layer around the `/mcp` nested service at `main.rs:105`. It accepts either `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (preferred) or `?token=<token>` (fallback, for MCP clients that can't set headers). Rejects with 401 on mismatch.
- Token-read helper lives in `panopt-core` (transport-agnostic) so both panoptd and the panopt launcher use the same code path.

### 4. Launcher wires the token in

- Update `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17` URL template. Preferred form moves the token into the MCP config's `headers` field (Claude Code's HTTP MCP config supports `headers`); URL param is the fallback if that proves brittle.
- Add `PANOPT_HOST`, `PANOPT_TOKEN`, `PANOPT_NAME` to the pane env in the launcher's spawn code (alongside the existing `PANOPT_WS`, `PANOPT_AGENT`, `PANOPT_PORT`).

### 5. `panopt agent-config` — the hand-launched on-ramp

Add a subcommand that emits the `--mcp-config` JSON for a hand-launched session:

```
panopt agent-config [--name <friendly>] [--id <stable-id>] [--host <addr>] [--port <port>]
```

Defaults: `id` = a generated stable per-user-machine id (e.g., `agent-<short-hash-of-hostname-plus-user>`); `name` = `$USER@$HOSTNAME` or similar legible default; `host`/`port` = daemon defaults; `token` read from the token file.

User flow:
```
claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name greg-main)"
```

Document this as the standard recipe in CLAUDE.md and README.md.

### 6. Touchpoint updates

- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs` — daemon-up probe respects `--host`.
- `crates/panopt/src/todo.rs` — observer URL respects `--host` and carries the token.
- `justfile` — `up`/`stop` recipes pass `--host` when overridden; `devagent` reads the token.
- `DESIGN.md` §5.3 (Transport) and §9 (Identity) — updated for configurable bind, bearer-token auth, URL-declared name.
- `CLAUDE.md` — add `panopt agent-config` as the standard hand-launch recipe.

## Critical files

- `crates/panoptd/src/main.rs` — `--host` flag, bind, middleware setup, token bootstrap.
- `crates/panoptd/src/handler.rs:448-490` — `agent_key`, `enter`; new `?name=` extraction and implicit `agent_identify`.
- `crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:86-103` — `identify` unchanged; called by `enter` when `?name=` is present.
- `crates/panopt-core/` (new helper) — token path/read function, shared by daemon and launcher.
- `crates/panopt/src/mcp.rs:17` — MCP URL template; move token to `headers` field.
- `crates/panopt/src/daemon.rs`, `crates/panopt/src/todo.rs` — host/token plumbing.

## Reuse

- `agent_key()` in `handler.rs:448` already does the stable-id-with-fallback dance. No change to its logic.
- `Store::agent_identify` already exists and is called by the `identify` tool handler. Reuse from `enter()` when `?name=` is present.
- `query_param()` helper in handler.rs already parses URL query params.
- `default_db_path()` at `main.rs:38` is the pattern for the per-user data-dir resolution the token path will follow.

## Verification

1. `just check && just clippy && just test` (workspace + plugin via the `verify-all` skill).
2. **Daemon bootstrap & auth.**
   - Fresh start: `just stop && just up` generates `~/.local/share/panopt/token` if absent.
   - `curl http://127.0.0.1:7600/mcp` with no auth → 401.
   - `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $(cat ~/.local/share/panopt/token)" …` → succeeds.
3. **Hand-launched first-class flow.**
   - `panopt agent-config --name greg-test` emits a JSON config.
   - `claude --mcp-config "$(panopt agent-config --name greg-test)"` opens a session.
   - From the spawned session: `whoami` returns `name=greg-test` (not a UUID).
   - From a second agent: `agent_list` shows `greg-test` stably across repeated polls — no rotation.
4. **Cross-machine (#80 acceptance).**
   - `just stop && panoptd --host 0.0.0.0 --port 7600` on host A.
   - Copy `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to host B.
   - On host B: `claude --mcp-config <config-with-host-A's-IP-and-token>` — successful tool calls.
   - On host A: agent appears in `agent_list` with its declared name and survives a transient reconnect.
5. **Lock retention under reconnect.**
   - From the hand-launched agent, acquire a lock.
   - Kill and restart the agent process with the same `agent-config` (same stable id).
   - The lock either survives (because the registry entry was idle but not yet swept) or is cleanly released — never silently transferred to a different key.

## Out of scope

- Per-project tokens, mTLS, token rotation UX. Bearer token is the v1 — leave room for `Authorization` header so we can swap in a stronger scheme later without breaking the URL form.
- Persisting agent names across daemon restarts. Names are still ephemeral; `?name=` re-applies on every reconnect, which is sufficient and matches the existing "registry is ephemeral by design" invariant in `crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:1-6`.
- Migrating in-flight agents (the launcher and existing panes will keep working with the loopback bind and need only the token wired in).

   - From the spawned session: `whoami` returns `name=greg-test` (not a UUID).
   - From a second agent: `agent_list` shows `greg-test` stably across repeated polls — no rotation.
4. **Cross-machine (#80 acceptance).**
   - `just stop && panoptd --host 0.0.0.0 --port 7600` on host A.
   - Copy `~/.local/share/panopt/token` to host B.
   - On host B: `claude --mcp-config <config-with-host-A's-IP-and-token>` — successful tool calls.
   - On host A: agent appears in `agent_list` with its declared name and survives a transient reconnect.
5. **Lock retention under reconnect.**
   - From the hand-launched agent, acquire a lock.
   - Kill and restart the agent process with the same `agent-config` (same stable id).
   - The lock either survives (because the registry entry was idle but not yet swept) or is cleanly released — never silently transferred to a different key.

## Out of scope

- Per-project tokens, mTLS, token rotation UX. Bearer token is the v1 — leave room for `Authorization` header so we can swap in a stronger scheme later without breaking the URL form.
- Persisting agent names across daemon restarts. Names are still ephemeral; `?name=` re-applies on every reconnect, which is sufficient and matches the existing "registry is ephemeral by design" invariant in `crates/panopt-core/src/registry.rs:1-6`.
- Migrating in-flight agents (the launcher and existing panes will keep working with the loopback bind and need only the token wired in).

content.content:
1	//! `panopt` - the PANopt cockpit launcher.
2	//!
3	//! Brings up the coordination daemon on demand and the Zellij cockpit, and
4	//! opens agent panes - each with a stable per-agent identity (DESIGN.md §9).
5	
6	mod agent;
7	mod agent_tool;
8	mod close_gate;
9	mod daemon;
10	mod delete_gate;
11	mod edit;
12	mod id_kind;
13	mod mcp;
14	mod mcpclient;
15	mod paths;
16	mod process;
17	mod scratchpad;
18	mod scratchpad_form;
19	mod todo;
20	mod todo_form;
21	mod up;
22	mod viewer;
23	mod viewstate;
24	mod wrap;
25	
26	use std::path::PathBuf;
27	
28	use clap::{Parser, Subcommand};
29	
30	#[derive(Parser)]
31	#[command(name = "panopt", about = "PANopt cockpit launcher", version)]
32	struct Cli {
33	    /// Port the panopt daemon listens on.
34	    #[arg(long, default_value_t = 7600, global = true)]
35	    port: u16,
36	
37	    #[command(subcommand)]
38	    command: Cmd,
39	}
40	
41	#[derive(Subcommand)]
42	enum Cmd {
43	    /// Boot the cockpit: the daemon, a Zellij session, the sidebar, and an
44	    /// agent. Attaches if the cockpit is already running.
45	    Up {
46	        /// Path to the panopt-zellij plugin `.wasm` (auto-detected from a dev
47	        /// build when omitted).
48	        #[arg(long)]
49	        plugin: Option<PathBuf>,
50	    },
51	    /// Open a new agent pane in the running cockpit.
52	    Agent {
53	        /// Optional readable name for the agent.
54	        name: Option<String>,
55	    },
56	    /// Inspect and edit the project's shared todos.
57	    Todo {
58	        /// Project root the todos belong to (default: the current directory).
59	        #[arg(long, global = true)]
60	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
61	        #[command(subcommand)]
62	        action: todo::TodoCmd,
63	    },
64	    /// Inspect and edit the project's agent tools (durable spawn configs).
65	    #[command(name = "agent-tool")]
66	    AgentTool {
67	        /// Project root the agent tools belong to (default: the current directory).
68	        #[arg(long, global = true)]
69	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
70	        #[command(subcommand)]
71	        action: agent_tool::AgentToolCmd,
72	    },
73	    /// Inspect and edit the project's processes (agent/command/terminal instances).
74	    Process {
75	        /// Project root the processes belong to (default: the current directory).
76	        #[arg(long, global = true)]
77	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
78	        #[command(subcommand)]
79	        action: process::ProcessCmd,
80	    },
81	    /// Operate on the project's scratchpads from the CLI. Currently exposes
82	    /// only `rm`; the rest of the surface lives in the cockpit's editor form.
83	    Scratchpad {
84	        /// Project root the scratchpads belong to (default: the current directory).
85	        #[arg(long, global = true)]
86	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
87	        #[command(subcommand)]
88	        action: scratchpad::ScratchpadCmd,
89	    },
90	    /// Resolve a numeric id to its resource kind (todo / scratchpad /
91	    /// agent-tool / process) via the daemon's `id_kind` MCP tool.
92	    #[command(name = "id-kind")]
93	    IdKind {
94	        /// Project root the id belongs to (default: the current directory).
95	        #[arg(long)]
96	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
97	        /// Numeric id to resolve.
98	        id: u64,
99	    },
100	    /// Internal: the entrypoint that runs inside an agent pane.
101	    #[command(name = "_agent", hide = true)]
102	    AgentExec {
103	        #[arg(long)]
104	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
105	        #[arg(long)]
106	        id: Option<String>,
107	    },
108	    /// Internal: start a process in this pane.
109	    #[command(name = "_process-run", hide = true)]
110	    ProcessRun {
111	        #[arg(long)]
112	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
113	        /// Numeric id of the process to start.
114	        id: u64,
115	    },
116	    /// Internal: a long-lived cockpit viewer pane.
117	    #[command(name = "_viewer", hide = true)]
118	    ViewerExec {
119	        #[arg(long)]
120	        ws: Option<PathBuf>,
121	        /// Routing-file token the sidebar plugin assigned this pane.
122	        #[arg(long)]
123	        slot: String,
124	        /// Initial item kind: todo, scratchpad, todo-list, scratchpad-list.
125	        #[arg(long)]
126	        kind: Option<String>,
127	        /// Initial item id, for the todo and scratchpad kinds.
128	        #[arg(long)]
129	        id: Option<u64>,
130	    },
131	    /// Internal: the floating close-gate dialog the sidebar plugin spawns
132	    /// when a destructive action would lose active items.
133	    #[command(name = "_close-gate", hide = true)]
134	    CloseGateExec {
135	        /// What the user tried to do: focus, tab, or quit.
136	        #[arg(long)]
137	        scope: String,
138	        /// Active items the dialog shows: `kind:label;kind:label;...`.
139	        #[arg(long, default_value = "")]
140	        items: String,
141	        /// Terminal pane id to close when scope is `focus`.
142	        #[arg(long)]
143	        target_pane: Option<u32>,
144	    },
145	    /// Internal: the floating delete-confirmation dialog the sidebar plugin
146	    /// spawns when the user presses `x` on a row.
147	    #[command(name = "_delete-gate", hide = true)]
148	    DeleteGateExec {
149	        /// Item kind: todo, scratchpad, agent-tool, or process.
150	        #[arg(long)]
151	        kind: String,
152	        /// Numeric id of the item the user wants to delete.
153	        #[arg(long)]
154	        id: u64,
155	        /// Human label for the row (title / name); shown in the dialog.
156	        #[arg(long, default_value = "")]
157	        label: String,
158	    },
159	}
160	
161	fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
162	    let cli = Cli::parse();
163	    match cli.command {
164	        Cmd::Up { plugin } => up::run(plugin, cli.port),
165	        Cmd::Agent { name } => agent::spawn(name),
166	        Cmd::Todo { ws, action } => todo::run(ws, action, cli.port),
167	        Cmd::AgentTool { ws, action } => agent_tool::run(ws, action, cli.port),
168	        Cmd::Process { ws, action } => process::run(ws, action, cli.port),
169	        Cmd::Scratchpad { ws, action } => scratchpad::run(ws, action, cli.port),
170	        Cmd::IdKind { ws, id } => id_kind::run(ws, id, cli.port),
171	        Cmd::AgentExec { ws, id } => agent::exec_in_pane(ws, id, cli.port),
172	        Cmd::ProcessRun { ws, id } => process::exec_entry(ws, id, cli.port),
173	        Cmd::ViewerExec { ws, slot, kind, id } => viewer::run(ws, cli.port, slot, kind, id),
174	        Cmd::CloseGateExec {
175	            scope,
176	            items,
177	            target_pane,
178	        } => close_gate::run(&scope, &items, target_pane, cli.port),
179	        Cmd::DeleteGateExec { kind, id, label } => delete_gate::run(&kind, id, &label, cli.port),
180	    }
181	}
182

1	[package]
2	name = "panopt-core"
3	version.workspace = true
4	edition.workspace = true
5	
6	# Deliberately minimal: no MCP, async, or HTTP crate. `rusqlite` is allowed - it
7	# is a synchronous embedded database, none of those three. `serde_json` is the
8	# one serialization crate, used only to store a todo's tag list as a JSON array
9	# column. `cargo tree -p panopt-core` is still expected to contain none of rmcp /
10	# axum / tokio - that is the mechanical check for the transport-agnostic-core
11	# principle.
12	[dependencies]
13	thiserror = { workspace = true }
14	serde_json = { workspace = true }
15	rusqlite = { version = "0.32", features = ["bundled"] }
16	
17	[dev-dependencies]
18	tempfile = "3"
19

[package]
name = "panopt-core"
version.workspace = true
edition.workspace = true

# Deliberately minimal: no MCP, async, or HTTP crate. `rusqlite` is allowed - it
# is a synchronous embedded database, none of those three. `serde_json` is the
# one serialization crate, used only to store a todo's tag list as a JSON array
# column. `cargo tree -p panopt-core` is still expected to contain none of rmcp /
# axum / tokio - that is the mechanical check for the transport-agnostic-core
# principle.
[dependencies]
thiserror = { workspace = true }
serde_json = { workspace = true }
rusqlite = { version = "0.32", features = ["bundled"] }

[dev-dependencies]
tempfile = "3"

addedBlocks:
## panopt
PANopt coordination daemon: shared todos and scratchpads across agents.
Each connection is scoped to one project by the ?ws=<project path> query parameter on the server URL.
- Registry: call identify (name, optional status) on startup so others can see you; whoami returns your own entry; agent_list returns every agent on the project. Connecting already registers you.
- Locks: lock_acquire (name, optional note) takes a named advisory lock and is non-blocking; lock_release frees it; lock_status lists every held lock. Locks are advisory - agents cooperate, the daemon does not enforce.
- Todos: todo_create (returns an id), todo_list (summaries), todo_get (one todo in full), todo_update (edit any field), todo_complete, todo_delete, todo_add_blocker / todo_remove_blocker / todo_set_blockers, todo_comment_add / todo_comment_update / todo_comment_delete, todo_tags_list (project tag vocabulary, union with scratchpads), and todo_lock / todo_unlock (advisory `todo:<id>` lock, surfaced as locked_by on todo_get). Each todo also projects to .panopt/todos/<id>.md.
- Scratchpads: scratchpad_create (returns an id), scratchpad_list, scratchpad_get (one scratchpad in full), scratchpad_append (add to body), scratchpad_read (body only), scratchpad_update (replace title, body, and/or tags), scratchpad_delete, scratchpad_tags_list (project tag vocabulary, same union as todo_tags_list). Each scratchpad also projects to .panopt/scratchpad/<id>.md.
- Agent tools (config layer): agent_tool_create (returns an id), agent_tool_list, agent_tool_get, agent_tool_update, agent_tool_delete. Durable per-project agent configurations the cockpit can spawn from. Projected to .panopt/agent_tools.md.
- Processes (instance layer): process_create (kind agent/command/terminal, optional agent_tool_id, returns an id), process_list, process_get, process_update, process_delete. Per-project process instances - the launchable agents, commands, and terminals the cockpit tracks. Deleting an agent tool nulls the agent_tool_id back-reference of any processes that re… [truncated]

1	//! The MCP server handler: the coordination tools and the `ServerHandler`
2	//! impl that advertises them.
3	//!
4	//! Every tool resolves its project from the `ws` query parameter on the
5	//! request URL (DESIGN.md Section 5.3) and registers the calling agent by its
6	//! agent key - the `agent` URL parameter when set, the MCP session id
7	//! otherwise - so one daemon serves every project at once with no per-session
8	//! state welded into the handler.
9

14	    Agent, AgentTool, AgentToolPatch, CoreError, Lock, Priority, Process, ProcessKind,
15	    ProcessPatch, ProjectId, Scratchpad, ScratchpadPatch, Store, Todo, TodoPatch, TodoStatus,
16	};
17	use rmcp::{
18	    handler::server::{common::Extension, router::tool::ToolRouter, wrapper::Parameters},
19	    model::*,
20	    tool, tool_handler, tool_router,
21	    ErrorData as McpError, ServerHandler,
22	};
23	use serde::Serialize;

26	    AgentToolCreateArgs, AgentToolDeleteArgs, AgentToolGetArgs, AgentToolUpdateArgs, IdKindArgs,

28	    ProcessGetArgs, ProcessUpdateArgs, ScratchpadAppendArgs, ScratchpadCreateArgs,

31	    TodoCompleteArgs, TodoCreateArgs, TodoDeleteArgs, TodoGetArgs, TodoLockArgs,

33	};

35	/// Per-session MCP handler.
36	///
37	/// rmcp builds one of these per MCP session via the factory in `main`. Each
38	/// holds a *clone of the shared `Arc`*, so every session - and therefore every
39	/// connected agent - mutates and reads the one `Mutex<Store>`. The session
40	/// carries no project: each tool call derives it from the request URL.
41	#[derive(Clone)]
42	pub struct Handler {
43	    state: Arc<Mutex<Store>>,
44	    // Part of rmcp's `#[tool_router]` / `#[tool_handler]` pattern: built once in
45	    // `new()` and consulted by the macro-generated `ServerHandler` impl. The
46	    // dead-code lint does not attribute that macro-generated use to the field.
47	    #[allow(dead_code)]
48	    tool_router: ToolRouter<Self>,
49	}

406	/// Find `key`'s value in a URL query string, percent-decoded.
407	///
408	/// `query` is the raw query component, with no leading `?`, as returned by
409	/// [`http::Uri::query`].
410	fn query_param(query: Option<&str>, key: &str) -> Option<String> {
411	    let prefix = format!("{key}=");
412	    let raw = query?
413	        .split('&')
414	        .find_map(|kv| kv.strip_prefix(prefix.as_str()))?;
415	    Some(
416	        percent_encoding::percent_decode_str(raw)
417	            .decode_utf8_lossy()
418	            .into_owned(),
419	    )
420	}
421	
422	/// Resolve the project for a request from the `ws` query parameter on the MCP
423	/// server URL.
424	///
425	/// Agents register PANopt per project with `?ws=<project path>` appended to the
426	/// URL, so the daemon scopes every call without inferring anything. The path is
427	/// percent-decoded, so workspace paths containing spaces survive the round trip.
428	fn resolve_project(store: &mut Store, parts: &Parts) -> Result<ProjectId, McpError> {
429	    let path = query_param(parts.uri.query(), "ws").ok_or_else(|| {
430	        McpError::invalid_params(
431	            "no project: the MCP server URL must end with ?ws=<absolute project path>",
432	            None,
433	        )
434	    })?;
435	    store
436	        .ensure_project(std::path::Path::new(&path))
437	        .map_err(map_core_err)
438	}
439	
440	/// The calling agent's stable key.
441	///
442	/// Prefers the `agent` query parameter on the MCP URL - a stable per-agent id
443	/// the launcher injects, which survives MCP session churn. Falls back to the
444	/// `mcp-session-id` header when no `agent` is set; that header rotates whenever
445	/// the client's connection drops and re-initializes, so without an `agent` id
446	/// one agent can briefly appear under several keys (see DESIGN.md Section 9).
447	/// `None` only for a request that carries neither.
448	fn agent_key(parts: &Parts) -> Option<String> {
449	    query_param(parts.uri.query(), "agent")
450	        .filter(|id| !id.is_empty())
451	        .or_else(|| {
452	            parts
453	                .headers
454	                .get("mcp-session-id")

1	//! The error type for `panopt-core`.
2	
3	use std::path::PathBuf;
4	
5	/// Errors returned by [`crate::Store`] operations.
6	#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
7	pub enum CoreError {
8	    /// No scratchpad exists with the given id in the project.
9	    #[error("scratchpad {0} not found")]
10	    ScratchpadNotFound(u64),
11	
12	    /// No todo exists with the given id in the project.
13	    #[error("todo {0} not found")]
14	    TodoNotFound(u64),
15	
16	    /// No comment exists with the given id on the named todo.
17	    #[error("todo {todo_id} has no comment {comment_id}")]
18	    TodoCommentNotFound { todo_id: u64, comment_id: u64 },
19	
20	    /// No agent tool exists with the given id in the project.
21	    #[error("agent tool {0} not found")]
22	    AgentToolNotFound(u64),
23	
24	    /// No process exists with the given id in the project.
25	    #[error("process {0} not found")]
26	    ProcessNotFound(u64),
27	
28	    /// A caller-supplied argument was rejected - for example, a todo asked to
29	    /// block itself. The message is safe to surface to the caller.
30	    #[error("{0}")]
31	    BadRequest(String),
32	
33	    /// No project row exists with the given internal id. Indicates a stale
34	    /// [`crate::ProjectId`] used after its row vanished - a bug, not a user
35	    /// error.
36	    #[error("project {0} not found")]
37	    ProjectNotFound(i64),
38	
39	    /// A project's workspace path does not exist or is not accessible, so its
40	    /// `.panopt/` projection cannot be located.
41	    #[error("workspace path not found or inaccessible: {0}")]
42	    Workspace(PathBuf),
43	
44	    /// The SQLite store returned an error.
45	    #[error("database error: {0}")]
46	    Db(#[from] rusqlite::Error),
47	
48	    /// A projected file could not be written. The committed database row is
49	    /// already authoritative; the daemon surfaces this rather than silently
50	    /// dropping it.
51	    #[error("projection i/o error: {0}")]
52	    Projection(#[from] std::io::Error),
53	}

serde_json = { workspace = true }
percent-encoding = "2"
# The `panopt todo edit` form TUI.
ratatui = "0.29"
crossterm = "0.28"
tui-textarea = "0.7"
# For measuring grapheme cell width when soft-wrapping the body field. Already
# in our dependency graph via ratatui; declaring it here lets us use it
# directly.
unicode-width = "0.2"

file.content:
# Default recipe: list everything.
default:
    @just --list

# Build the workspace (debug).
build:
    cargo build --workspace

# Build the workspace (release).
release:
    cargo build --workspace --release

# Build the Zellij sidebar plugin (wasm32-wasip1).
plugin:
    cargo build --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml --target wasm32-wasip1

# Build the Zellij sidebar plugin in release mode.
plugin-release:
    cargo build --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml --target wasm32-wasip1 --release

# Run workspace tests.
test:
    cargo test --workspace

# Format all crates (workspace + plugin).
fmt:
    cargo fmt --all
    cargo fmt --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml

# Check formatting without modifying files.
fmt-check:
    cargo fmt --all -- --check
    cargo fmt --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml -- --check

# Clippy across the workspace and the plugin, warnings as errors.
clippy:
    cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings
    cargo clippy --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml --target wasm32-wasip1 -- -D warnings

# Fast type-check (workspace + plugin).
check:
    cargo check --workspace
    cargo check --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml --target wasm32-wasip1

# Install panopt + panoptd to ~/.cargo/bin.
install:
    cargo install --path crates/panopt
    cargo install --path crates/panoptd

# Run the daemon in the foreground (logs to stderr).
daemon:
    cargo run -p panoptd -- --port 7600

# Tail the daemon log file written by `panopt up`.
logs:
    tail -F ~/.local/share/panopt/panoptd.log

# If a daemon is already running on port 7600, `panopt up` connects to it
# rather than restarting it - use `just stop && just up` to force a fresh
# debug daemon, or `just restart-daemon` to swap the daemon out without
# touching the cockpit.
#
# Launch the cockpit in the current project (debug binaries). Build the
# workspace AND the release wasm plugin first so `panoptd` and the sidebar
# plugin both reflect the latest sources; without these, `cargo run -p
# panopt` would happily re-spawn yesterday's `panoptd` from `target/debug/`
# and Zellij would load yesterday's plugin from
# `crates/panopt-zellij/target/wasm32-wasip1/release/panopt-zellij.wasm`
# (the path `panopt up` auto-detects in `default_plugin_wasm`).
up: plugin-release
    cargo build --workspace
    cargo run -p panopt -- up

# Stop the running panoptd (SIGTERM x2 to clear the clients-connected gate).
stop:
    -pkill -TERM -x panoptd
    @sleep 1
    -pkill -TERM -x panoptd

# Nuke EVERYTHING and start over: kill every panopt-* Zellij session (which
# tears down the loaded wasm plugin and every `_viewer` / `_agent` /
# `_process-run` subprocess), kill the daemon, rebuild workspace + release
# wasm, then `panopt up`. Use when a code change spans the daemon, the
# binaries, and/or the plugin and you want the running cockpit to actually
# reflect HEAD. Must be run from a plain shell (not inside Zellij).
refresh:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail
    if [ -n "${ZELLIJ:-}" ]; then
        echo "run \`just refresh\` from a plain shell, not inside Zellij" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    # `grep` exits 1 when no panopt-* sessions match - that's a clean slate,
    # not an error. Wrap so `set -o pipefail` doesn't abort the recipe when
    # there's nothing to kill (e.g. a follow-up refresh after a crash).
    zellij list-sessions -sn 2>/dev/null | { grep '^panopt-' || true; } | while read -r s; do
        zellij kill-session "$s" 2>/dev/null || true
        zellij delete-session "$s" 2>/dev/null || true
    done
    pkill -TERM -x panoptd 2>/dev/null || true
    sleep 1
    pkill -TERM -x panoptd 2>/dev/null || true
    just up

# Rebuild panoptd, stop the running daemon, and re-launch it detached. The
# cockpit's MCP clients reconnect on the next call, so this is the
# ergonomic path while a cockpit is open: edit handler code, `just
# restart-daemon`, the next autosave hits the new binary.
restart-daemon:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail
    cargo build -p panoptd
    pkill -TERM -x panoptd 2>/dev/null || true
    sleep 1
    pkill -TERM -x panoptd 2>/dev/null || true
    sleep 1
    mkdir -p "$HOME/.local/share/panopt"
    setsid -f target/debug/panoptd --port 7600 \
        < /dev/null \
        >> "$HOME/.local/share/panopt/panoptd.log" 2>&1
    for _ in $(seq 1 50); do
        if ss -ltn 2>/dev/null | grep -q '127.0.0.1:7600'; then
            echo "panoptd restarted on 127.0.0.1:7600"
            exit 0
        fi
        sleep 0.1
    done
    echo "panoptd did not start listening within 5s; see ~/.local/share/panopt/panoptd.log" >&2
    exit 1

# Open the panopt sqlite database in the sqlite3 shell.
db:
    sqlite3 ~/.local/share/panopt/panopt.db

# Clean both target dirs.
clean:
    cargo clean
    cargo clean --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml

# run a 'connected' agent externally (outside of panopt). this is for connecting a dev-time agent to the built system
devagent:
    claude --mcp-config '{"mcpServers":{"panopt":{"type":"http","url":"http://127.0.0.1:7600/mcp?ws='$PWD'"}}}'

devagent:
    claude --mcp-config "$(cargo run -q -p panopt -- agent-config)"

# restart-daemon`, the next autosave hits the new binary.
restart-daemon:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail
    cargo build -p panoptd
    pkill -TERM -x panoptd 2>/dev/null || true
    sleep 1
    pkill -TERM -x panoptd 2>/dev/null || true
    sleep 1
    mkdir -p "$HOME/.local/share/panopt"
    setsid -f target/debug/panoptd --port 7600 \
        < /dev/null \
        >> "$HOME/.local/share/panopt/panoptd.log" 2>&1
    for _ in $(seq 1 50); do
        if ss -ltn 2>/dev/null | grep -q '127.0.0.1:7600'; then
            echo "panoptd restarted on 127.0.0.1:7600"
            exit 0
        fi
        sleep 0.1
    done
    echo "panoptd did not start listening within 5s; see ~/.local/share/panopt/panoptd.log" >&2
    exit 1

# Open the panopt sqlite database in the sqlite3 shell.
db:
    sqlite3 ~/.local/share/panopt/panopt.db

# Clean both target dirs.
clean:
    cargo clean
    cargo clean --manifest-path crates/panopt-zellij/Cargo.toml

# run a 'connected' agent externally (outside of panopt). this is for connecting a dev-time agent to the built system
devagent:
    claude --mcp-config '{"mcpServers":{"panopt":{"type":"http","url":"http://127.0.0.1:7600/mcp?ws='$PWD'"}}}'

+# Uses `panopt agent-config` so the launched session gets a stable agent id,
+# a friendly display name, and the bearer token the daemon requires.

### 5.3 Transport

The daemon listens on **HTTP/SSE (Streamable HTTP) on localhost**, not stdio.

Rationale: stdio MCP is one-client-per-process. If agents used stdio, each
would spawn its own private copy of the daemon with no shared state, which
defeats the entire purpose. A shared coordination hub requires one daemon
serving many connections, which means a network transport. A unix-domain
socket is a reasonable hardening option later, but localhost HTTP is chosen
first because agent CLIs configure HTTP MCP servers by URL with no friction.

There is one daemon instance, and it serves every project at once. A project
is selected per connection by a `ws` query parameter on the MCP URL:
`http://127.0.0.1:PORT/mcp?ws=<absolute project path>`. An agent's `claude mcp
add`, run inside the project, captures that path from `$PWD`, so registration
is the moment the project is named - the daemon never has to guess. The daemon
canonicalizes the path, so symlinks and trailing slashes collapse onto one
project, and distinct git worktrees are distinct projects unless deliberately
pointed at the same path. The URL may also carry an `agent` parameter - a
stable per-agent id used as the registry key (Sections 6.3 and 9). Every
project's state lives in one SQLite database the daemon owns (Section 6.4).

### 5.4 Coordination plane (agents to daemon)

This plane does not involve the host at all.

Each agent CLI is configured with PANopt as an MCP server in its own
configuration (for example, `claude mcp add --transport http panopt
http://127.0.0.1:PORT/mcp?ws=$PWD`, run inside the project). Zellij is merely
the process that launched the terminal; it is not in the loop.

An agent is registered with the daemon automatically: the first tool call on a
connection adds it to the agent registry (Section 6.3), keyed by its connection
key. The `identify` tool then enriches that entry with a human name and
status, and `whoami` / `agent_list` read it back. An agent gains the
coordination tools - `todo_*`, `scratchpad_*`, the registry tools, and `lock_*`
- simply by connecting. Because this plane is pure MCP, it works
identically with any MCP-capable agent and any host - Zellij today, a bare
shell, or anything else later. The coordination core is never welded to the
host.

### 5.5 Presentation plane (daemon to the cockpit)

Two mechanisms surface the daemon's state - one host-agnostic, one
Zellij-native:

1. **Filesystem projection (the backbone).** On every state mutation the daemon
   atomically rewrites markdown files under `.panopt/` (`todos.md`,
   `scratchpad/<id>.md`) - write to a temp file, then rename, so a reader never
   sees a half-written file. Any editor with live file-reload renders them as

content.content:
380	to list them). Because scratchpads are append-mostly, bidirectional sync is
381	clean: a user editing in any editor and an agent appending via MCP both land,
382	and the daemon reconciles by section. Appends are conflict-free by construction.
383	
384	### 6.3 Agent registry and locks
385	
386	The registry tracks which agents are currently connected, scoped per project.
387	Each agent is keyed by a connection key and carries a name and a free-form
388	status, both set through the `identify` tool; `whoami` returns the caller's own
389	entry and `agent_list` returns every agent on the project. The first tool call
390	on any connection registers the agent, so even one that never calls `identify`
391	still appears.
392	
393	The connection key is the `agent` query parameter on the MCP URL when one is
394	set - a stable per-agent id - and the MCP session id otherwise. The session-id
395	fallback is imperfect: a Streamable HTTP session is scoped to a connection
396	episode, so it rotates whenever a client reconnects, and without an `agent` id
397	one agent then splits across several keys until the idle ones are pruned (see
398	Section 9).
399	
400	The registry is in-memory only, never persisted: it describes *currently
401	connected* agents, so a daemon restart correctly forgets it and lets it refill
402	as agents reconnect. An agent that has made no tool call for five minutes is
403	pruned as gone; a background sweep in the daemon runs that prune every 30
404	seconds, so a closed agent leaves the registry even when no other agent is
405	active to trigger one. The registry is projected to `.panopt/agents.md` like
406	every other piece of state.
407	
408	Locks are advisory: a lock is a named claim one agent holds so others
409	coordinate exclusive work voluntarily - the daemon records the holder but
410	enforces nothing. `lock_acquire` takes a lock and is non-blocking (it reports
411	the current holder rather than waiting), `lock_release` frees it, and
412	`lock_status` lists every lock held in the project. Like the registry, locks
413	are in-memory and ephemeral, and a lock is released automatically when its
414	holder is pruned. The table is projected to `.panopt/locks.md`.
415	
416	### 6.4 Persistence
417	
418	A single SQLite database holds every project. One database file is simpler to
419	operate than a file per project, and SQLite gives durability and queryability
420	with no server.
421	
422	Five tables: `projects`, `todos`, `scratchpads`, `agent_tools`, and `processes`
423	(Section 6.6). The latter four are keyed by `(project_id, id)`, so ids restart
424	at 1 in each project and read naturally in the projected files. A single
425	per-project `next_id` counter on the `projects` row is shared across all four
426	resource types and bumped in the same transaction as the insert, so an id is
427	never handed out twice, never reused after a deletion, and a `#N` reference
428	points to exactly one resource. Each mutation commits its transaction and then
429	re-projects the affected `.panopt/` file; the first time a project is touched
430	in a daemon run, every file is re-projected from the database, which both
431	initializes a new project and self-heals a restarted one. The database file
432	lives in the per-user data directory (`panopt/panopt.db`); the daemon owns it,
433	and no project ever sees it.
434	
435	### 6.5 Conflict model
436	
437	The daemon is the single source of truth. Projected files are derived state.
438	
439	- Scratchpads: section-based merge; appends never conflict.

### 6.3 Agent registry and locks

The registry tracks which agents are currently connected, scoped per project.
Each agent is keyed by a connection key and carries a name and a free-form
status, both set through the `identify` tool; `whoami` returns the caller's own
entry and `agent_list` returns every agent on the project. The first tool call
on any connection registers the agent, so even one that never calls `identify`
still appears.

The connection key is the `agent` query parameter on the MCP URL when one is
set - a stable per-agent id - and the MCP session id otherwise. The session-id
fallback is imperfect: a Streamable HTTP session is scoped to a connection
episode, so it rotates whenever a client reconnects, and without an `agent` id
one agent then splits across several keys until the idle ones are pruned (see
Section 9).

The registry is in-memory only, never persisted: it describes *currently
connected* agents, so a daemon restart correctly forgets it and lets it refill
as agents reconnect. An agent that has made no tool call for five minutes is
pruned as gone; a background sweep in the daemon runs that prune every 30
seconds, so a closed agent leaves the registry even when no other agent is
active to trigger one. The registry is projected to `.panopt/agents.md` like
every other piece of state.

Locks are advisory: a lock is a named claim one agent holds so others
coordinate exclusive work voluntarily - the daemon records the holder but
enforces nothing. `lock_acquire` takes a lock and is non-blocking (it reports
the current holder rather than waiting), `lock_release` frees it, and
`lock_status` lists every lock held in the project. Like the registry, locks
are in-memory and ephemeral, and a lock is released automatically when its
holder is pruned. The table is projected to `.panopt/locks.md`.

### 6.4 Persistence

A single SQLite database holds every project. One database file is simpler to
operate than a file per project, and SQLite gives durability and queryability
with no server.

Five tables: `projects`, `todos`, `scratchpads`, `agent_tools`, and `processes`
(Section 6.6). The latter four are keyed by `(project_id, id)`, so ids restart
at 1 in each project and read naturally in the projected files. A single
per-project `next_id` counter on the `projects` row is shared across all four
resource types and bumped in the same transaction as the insert, so an id is
never handed out twice, never reused after a deletion, and a `#N` reference
points to exactly one resource. Each mutation commits its transaction and then
re-projects the affected `.panopt/` file; the first time a project is touched
in a daemon run, every file is re-projected from the database, which both
initializes a new project and self-heals a restarted one. The database file
lives in the per-user data directory (`panopt/panopt.db`); the daemon owns it,
and no project ever sees it.

### 6.5 Conflict model

The daemon is the single source of truth. Projected files are derived state.

- Scratchpads: section-based merge; appends never conflict.

content.content:
547	## 9. Risks and Open Questions
548	
549	- Editor live-reload of the projected files - the load-bearing assumption of
550	  the presentation plane. Verified in POC 1 on macOS with Zed.
551	- Zellij plugin-API maturity and version coupling. A plugin is compiled against
552	  a specific `zellij-tile` version; a Zellij upgrade can require recompiling it.
553	- Ergonomics of bidirectional todo editing. If write-back proves fiddly, todos
554	  may degrade gracefully to a read-only projected view plus command-driven
555	  editing.
556	- Daemon lifecycle: decided - on-demand launch. The first agent (or the Zellij
557	  layout) starts `panoptd` if nothing is already listening on its port; one
558	  global daemon then serves every project. There is no systemd unit or other
559	  system integration, so the daemon behaves identically on macOS and Linux. The
560	  `panopt` launcher performs that start-if-absent check, starting `panoptd`
561	  detached in its own session so it outlives the launching terminal and every
562	  Zellij session. The daemon also runs a two-strike SIGTERM guard: the first
563	  shutdown signal is refused if any MCP client is still connected (the daemon
564	  logs the per-project agent count and keeps serving); a second signal within
565	  a short confirmation window exits. SIGKILL bypasses this by design.
566	- Agent identification: solved. Project selection is explicit (the `ws` URL
567	  parameter, Section 5.3). The MCP session id alone is unreliable as an agent
568	  key - a Streamable HTTP session is connection-episode-scoped and rotates when
569	  the client reconnects - so the daemon keys an agent on a stable `agent` query
570	  parameter when the URL carries one, falling back to the session id otherwise.
571	  The `panopt` launcher gives each agent pane a unique `agent` id, via a
572	  per-pane `PANOPT_AGENT` environment variable that the agent's MCP config
573	  expands into the URL, so an agent keeps one identity across session churn.
574	
575	## 10. Out of Scope and Future Work
576	
577	- The remainder of Solo's large tool surface (timers, prompt templates, process
578	  spawn and management, services). Added incrementally after the POC.
579	- Process spawning and supervision. Solo spawns and manages processes itself;
580	  PANopt initially relies on Zellij to host agent and command panes, and may
581	  add direct process management later.
